Q1: I kept things pretty simple with my study routine. I'd usually study around 22 hours a week, spread out across different days, but I'm the type who learns best by doing things with my hands. I'd often work through problems physically, write things out, or practice concepts by building or creating something tangible. I didn't have the most structured schedule, but I made sure to put in consistent effort throughout the semester.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I tried to keep some regularity, but honestly, when deadlines approached or when I had extracurricular commitments, my study times would shift around. I'm not someone who can stick to the exact same schedule every week—life just doesn't work that way for me, especially with limited resources to work with.
Q3: I'd log into the LMS mainly to check announcements, see if there were new materials posted, or to track what assignments were coming up. Sometimes the gamified tasks would pull me in because they made things feel less like a chore. I logged in pretty frequently—probably around 28 times over the course—just to stay on top of things and not miss anything important.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became pretty automatic. I'd wake up, check my phone, and logging into the LMS was just part of that routine. The gamified elements actually helped because there was this sense of progress and small rewards that kept me coming back. It wasn't forced; it just became a habit like checking messages.
Q5: The videos were really helpful for me—I watched over 70 minutes of them. Since I'm a kinesthetic learner, seeing things demonstrated helped me understand how to actually do them. I also appreciated the discussion posts because even though I only posted a few times, reading what others said helped me see different perspectives on the material.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't complete all the assignments—my completion rate was pretty low. Sometimes the readings felt too abstract without any hands-on component, so I'd skim them or skip them if I thought I could learn the same thing through practice or videos. When resources didn't connect to something practical, I tended to put them aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to actually understanding or applying the material. The gamified tasks were great because they gave me immediate feedback and made learning feel like a challenge to overcome. If something felt like busywork with no clear purpose, I'd lose interest quickly.
Q8: Optional activities felt more relaxed, like I could engage with them on my own terms without pressure. Required ones sometimes felt like boxes to check, which could be demotivating. But honestly, I sometimes put more genuine effort into optional activities because I was choosing to do them, not being forced.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then study from them offline because my internet access isn't always reliable and I don't have a lot of resources to keep data running constantly. I'd also use physical notes and practice problems on paper—that hands-on approach works better for my learning style anyway.
Q10: There were many times I was studying intensely but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd print out materials and work through them at the library or at home without being online. Sometimes I'd study with a couple of peers in person, discussing concepts and quizzing each other. None of that showed up in any log.
Q11: Yes, definitely. I'd access a video or document once, download it or take detailed notes, and then never open it again on the LMS. My exam score was really high because I was studying that material—just not in a way the system could track. I'd review my notes multiple times offline.
Q12: There were a few weeks when my extracurricular activities got really intense and I had to prioritize. My attendance dropped a bit during those times. I also had some moments where motivation was low—not stressed or anxious, just not feeling driven. Those periods made it harder to stay engaged with online content.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage. When I had extracurricular commitments or personal stuff to handle, I'd do the minimum online and focus my studying offline where I could be more efficient. Fatigue was sometimes a factor too, but I managed to keep my stress pretty low overall.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was elsewhere. I'd be technically logged in and watching, but not really absorbing anything. Those moments usually happened when I was tired or just going through the motions before a deadline.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have. My assignment completion was pretty low because I'd prioritize studying for exams or doing hands-on practice instead. I knew the assignments were important, but when time was limited, I focused on what I felt would help my actual understanding and exam performance more.
Q16: Deadlines definitely pushed me to log in more and check what was due. I'd plan my study sessions around upcoming deadlines, cramming in LMS activity when something was approaching. Without deadlines, I might have been more relaxed about checking in, but they kept me accountable.
Q17: Feedback from the gamified tasks was motivating because it was immediate and showed me where I stood. When I got positive feedback, it encouraged me to keep going. If feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose some momentum. The quick response from gamified elements really helped keep me engaged.
Q18: The course structure influenced me a lot. Having clear modules and knowing what was expected each week helped me plan. The gamified components added variety and made the structure feel less rigid. When the structure was clear, I could manage my time better despite my other commitments.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier—they broke things into smaller, achievable goals which suited my style. What made it harder was when content was purely reading-based with no interactive component. I thrive when I can do something, not just read about it.
Q20: I think that happens when students are exploring or trying to understand the system but struggling with the actual assignments. Maybe they're watching videos and reading but can't translate that into completed work due to time constraints, confusion, or personal circumstances. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. My LMS activity wasn't always super high, especially for task completion, but I scored really well on exams. I was learning effectively offline through hands-on practice and notes. The LMS only captures part of the picture—it misses all the studying that happens away from the screen.
Q22: Course logs can show login frequency, time spent on videos, and submission patterns. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, peer discussions, and whether someone is actually understanding the material. My high exam score came from deep learning that happened mostly outside the LMS.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's about being mentally present, asking questions, and connecting concepts to real applications. For me, engagement is hands-on—working through problems, discussing with peers, and genuinely trying to master the content.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied from printed notes, the peer study sessions I had, or the hands-on practice I did to really understand concepts. It also wouldn't capture my mindset—that even with low motivation sometimes, I pushed through because I wanted to do well. My distinction grade came from effort the system couldn't see.
